title: Research
text: My research focuses on Machine Learning and Statistical Learning applied to biomedical, health-related and social-science data. I am interested in developing and applying statistical models, machine learning algorithms and data mining methods from both methodological and applied perspectives.

title: Research
text:
Research interests

Non-parametric and ensemble models
A central part of my research concerns non-parametric and ensemble learning methods, with particular attention to robust modelling strategies for complex data structures.


Survival analysis
I work on statistical and machine learning models for censored data, including ensemble approaches for survival outcomes and biomedical applications.


Clustering and Bayesian approaches
My research includes clustering methods, Bayesian ensemble approaches, robust similarity measures and methods for high-dimensional and complex data.


Biomedical and neuroimaging applications
I collaborate on projects involving neurosciences, neuroimaging, radiomics, neurological diseases and biomedical data analysis.


Social-science and health data
I also apply statistical and computational methods to social-science data, including education, socio-economic topics, health data and questionnaire-based research.

title: Research
text:
Keywords
Machine learning · Statistical learning · Survival analysis · Ensemble models · Clustering · Bayesian methods · Biomedical statistics · Neuroimaging · Health data · Social-science data

title: Current Open Topics
text: This page presents a selection of current research directions and open methodological questions that I am actively developing. The aim is to highlight ongoing topics rather than provide a complete overview of past research activities.

title: Current Open Topics
text:
EEG clustering through tensor decomposition
EEG data are naturally multi-dimensional, combining subjects, channels, time, frequencies and experimental conditions. Tensor decomposition offers a way to preserve this multiway structure, but the connection between decomposition quality and clustering performance remains an open methodological issue.
Research question: How can tensor decomposition methods be designed or regularized to obtain meaningful and stable clusters of EEG profiles?

title: Current Open Topics
text:
Clustering individual networks
In several biomedical and cognitive applications, each individual can be represented by a subject-specific network. The methodological challenge is different from classical community detection: the goal is not to identify communities within a single network, but to cluster individuals according to the structure of their networks.
Research question: How can subject-specific networks be compared and clustered while preserving interpretability and avoiding circularity in the estimation process?

title: Current Open Topics
section: Bayesian ensemble methods for clustering and prediction
text:
Bayesian ensemble methods for clustering and prediction
Ensemble methods are powerful tools for improving stability and predictive performance, but their uncertainty is often difficult to quantify and communicate. Bayesian and bootstrap-based approaches provide a promising framework to combine robustness, uncertainty assessment and interpretability.
Research question: How can Bayesian ensemble methods produce stable predictions or partitions while providing meaningful uncertainty information?

title: Current Open Topics
text:
Robust rank-based clustering
Many clustering problems involve data structures for which classical metric assumptions are too restrictive or unstable. Rank-based information offers a flexible way to compare observations or complex objects while reducing the impact of scale, noise and distributional assumptions.
Research question: How can rank-based similarity measures be used to build robust clustering procedures for complex and heterogeneous data?

title: Methodological and Computational Approaches in Data Science

Course: Methodological and Computational Approaches in Data Science
Level: Master degree
Degree programme: SERI — Economic Development and International Relations
Department: Department of Political and Social Sciences, University of Pavia

title: Methodological and Computational Approaches in Data Science
text:
Course overview
This course introduces students to the methodological and computational foundations of data science. It combines theoretical lessons with hands-on sessions in R, with the aim of developing both conceptual understanding and practical skills. The course starts from the general meaning of data science and from the use of R for data exploration, then moves toward supervised and unsupervised learning methods.
The methodological part covers regression models, classification methods, error estimation, ensemble models, clustering, market basket analysis, neural networks and text mining. The course also emphasizes how to read, interpret and present scientific articles that apply machine learning models to real-world problems, encouraging students to connect data science techniques with substantive research questions and applied evidence.

title: Methodological and Computational Approaches in Data Science
text:
Materials
Slides, recordings, code, notices and additional course materials are available through the University of Pavia Kiro e-learning platform: https://elearning.unipv.it/.

title: Methodological and Computational Approaches in Data Science
text:
Suggested readings
The following books are suggested as complementary readings for students interested in data science, machine learning and the broader social impact of data-driven methods. They are not mandatory course materials. Not all readings have been personally verified by me.

Gareth James, Daniela Witten, Trevor Hastie, Robert Tibshirani and Jonathan Taylor, An Introduction to Statistical Learning: With Applications in R.
Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel and Garrett Grolemund, R for Data Science.
Foster Provost and Tom Fawcett, Data Science for Business.
John D. Kelleher and Brendan Tierney, Data Science.
Hannah Fry, Hello World: Being Human in the Age of Algorithms.
Cathy O’Neil, Weapons of Math Destruction.
Brian Christian, The Alignment Problem: Machine Learning and Human Values.

Department: Department of Political and Social Sciences, University of Pavia

title: Data Driven Approach for Social Sciences
text:
Course overview
This course introduces the basic statistical tools needed to read, describe and interpret quantitative information in the social sciences. Starting from probability and elementary set operations, the course gradually moves toward descriptive statistics, random variables, estimation, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing and simple bivariate methods. The aim is to help students develop statistical reasoning and use data analysis as a transparent support for social-science questions.
The course gives particular attention to the interpretation of empirical results: how variables are defined, how data can be summarized graphically and numerically, how uncertainty can be quantified, and how basic statistical tests can be used to compare groups or study associations between variables. Introductory elements of linear regression are also discussed as a first step toward model-based reasoning.

title: Data Driven Approach for Social Sciences
text:
Materials
Slides, notices and course materials are available through the University of Pavia Kiro e-learning platform: https://elearning.unipv.it/.

title: Data Driven Approach for Social Sciences
text:
Suggested readings
The following books are suggested as complementary readings for students interested in developing a broader view of statistics, data and quantitative reasoning. They are not mandatory course materials. Not all readings have been personally verified by me.

David Spiegelhalter, The Art of Statistics: Learning from Data.
Charles Wheelan, Naked Statistics: Stripping the Dread from the Data.
Darrell Huff, How to Lie with Statistics.
Alberto Cairo, How Charts Lie: Getting Smarter about Visual Information.
Hans Rosling, Ola Rosling and Anna Rosling Rönnlund, Factfulness: Ten Reasons We’re Wrong About the World — and Why Things Are Better Than You Think.

title: Home
text:
Assistant Professor in Statistics
Department of Political and Social Sciences
University of Pavia
I am a statistician working on machine learning and statistical learning methods for biomedical, health-related and social-science data. My research combines methodological development and applied data science, with interests in non-parametric and ensemble models, survival analysis, clustering, neuroimaging and statistical models for social sciences.

Publications Research Download CV


Google Scholar · ORCID · GitHub · LinkedIn

title: Home
text:
Research at a glance


Statistical Learning
Development and application of statistical learning methods, with attention to interpretability, robustness and real-world data constraints.


Biomedical Data Science
Machine learning and data mining for biomedical data, neuroimaging, neurosciences and health-related applications.


Social and Health Data
Statistical and computational models for social sciences, education, socio-economic topics and health data.
